perturbation.original_text: Each of the parties agrees that from time to time, at the reasonable request and expense of the other party, it shall execute and deliver such other documents and take such other actions as the other party may reasonably request to effectuate the transactions contemplated by this IP Agreement (including any short form documentation evidencing the licenses granted by any Seller Party hereunder or other documentation to perfect or record the rights granted hereunder in the Transferred IP or Licensed IP in any jurisdiction throughout the world). The Seller acknowledges and agrees (including on behalf of the other Seller Parties) that the Purchaser or any of its Affiliates may record and perfect this IP Agreement or such documentation in any jurisdiction throughout the world, and the Seller shall (and shall cause the other Seller Parties to) cooperate therewith, at the Purchaser's expense. The Purchaser hereby requests, and the Seller hereby grants (and shall cause the other Seller Parties to grant) to the Purchaser and its Affiliates, all rights necessary to record this IP Agreement or such documentation with the United States Patent and Trademark Office, the United States Copyright Office, and any equivalent office or agency in any jurisdiction in the world. Seller shall (and shall cause the other Seller Parties) to, between the Effective Date and the Initial Closing Date, cooperate with the Purchaser in connection with the Purchaser's preparation for acquiring the Business, including (i) by reasonably sharing information to prevent any loss of any of the Seller Entities' rights to any Intellectual Property constituting Transferred IP or Licensed IP, and (ii) facilitating between the parties and their respective Affiliates' communication and sharing of information related to this IP Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the parties agrees that from time to time, at the reasonable request and expense of the other party, it shall execute and deliver such other documents and take such other actions as the other party may reasonably request to effectuate the transactions contemplated by this IP Agreement (including any short form documentation evidencing the licenses granted by any Seller Party hereunder or other documentation to perfect or record the rights granted hereunder in the Transferred IP or Licensed IP in any jurisdiction throughout the world). The Seller acknowledges and agrees (including on behalf of the other Seller Parties) that the Purchaser or any of its Affiliates may record and perfect this IP Agreement or such documentation in any jurisdiction throughout the world, and the Seller shall (and shall cause the other Seller Parties to) cooperate therewith, at the Purchaser's expense. The Purchaser hereby requests, and the Seller hereby grants (and shall cause the other Seller Parties to grant) to the Purchaser and its Affiliates, all rights necessary to record this IP Agreement or such documentation with the United States Patent and Trademark Office, the United States Copyright Office, and any equivalent office or agency in any jurisdiction in the world. Seller shall (and shall cause the other Seller Parties) to, between the Effective Date and the Initial Closing Date, cooperate with the Purchaser in connection with the Purchaser's preparation for acquiring the Business, including (i) by reasonably sharing information to prevent any loss of any of the Seller Entities' rights to any Intellectual Property constituting Transferred IP or Licensed IP, and (ii) facilitating between the parties and their respective Affiliates' communication and sharing of information related to this IP Agreement, **except for the cost associated with registering at the United States Copyright Office, which will be at the Purchaser's expense.**
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation introduces an in-text contradiction regarding who bears the expense of recording at the United States Copyright Office. While the original text states the Purchaser bears all expenses related to recording and perfecting the IP Agreement, the added clause shifts the cost of registration at the United States Copyright Office to the Purchaser, creating a conflict. It's uncertain whether 'at the Purchaser's expense' applies to all costs in this Section 6.5 or if the modified text means the Seller now bears all costs except for registration at the US Copyright Office.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each of the parties agrees that from time to time, at the reasonable request and expense of the other party, it shall execute and deliver such other documents and take such other actions as the other party may reasonably request to effectuate the transactions contemplated by this IP Agreement (including any short form documentation evidencing the licenses granted by any Seller Party hereunder or other documentation to perfect or record the rights granted hereunder in the Transferred IP or Licensed IP in any jurisdiction throughout the world). The Seller acknowledges and agrees (including on behalf of the other Seller Parties) that the Purchaser or any of its Affiliates may record and perfect this IP Agreement or such documentation in any jurisdiction throughout the world, and the Seller shall (and shall cause the other Seller Parties to) cooperate therewith, at the Purchaser's expense. The Purchaser hereby requests, and the Seller hereby grants (and shall cause the other Seller Parties to grant) to the Purchaser and its Affiliates, all rights necessary to record this IP Agreement or such documentation with the United States Patent and Trademark Office, the United States Copyright Office, and any equivalent office or agency in any jurisdiction in the world. Seller shall (and shall cause the other Seller Parties) to, between the Effective Date and the Initial Closing Date, cooperate with the Purchaser in connection with the Purchaser's preparation for acquiring the Business, including (i) by reasonably sharing information to prevent any loss of any of the Seller Entities' rights to any Intellectual Property constituting Transferred IP or Licensed IP, and (ii) facilitating between the parties and their respective Affiliates' communication and sharing of information related to this IP Agreement, except for the cost associated with registering at the United States Copyright Office, which will be at the Purchaser's expense.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an inconsistency regarding who bears the expense of recording at the United States Copyright Office. The original text within the contradicted section states the Purchaser bears all expenses related to recording and perfecting the IP Agreement ('the Seller shall (and shall cause the other Seller Parties to) cooperate therewith, at the Purchaser's expense.'). The added clause in the changed text shifts the cost of registration at the United States Copyright Office to the Purchaser ('except for the cost associated with registering at the United States Copyright Office, which will be at the Purchaser's expense.'), creating a direct conflict about cost allocation for this specific task, which falls under 'recording and perfecting this IP Agreement or such documentation'.

perturbation.original_text: The parties may amend this IP Agreement only by a written agreement signed by the parties and that identifies itself as an amendment to this IP Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties may amend this IP Agreement only by a written agreement signed by the parties and that identifies itself as an amendment to this IP Agreement, **unless there is mutual verbal agreement made between both parties.**
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation introduces an in-text contradiction regarding how the IP agreement can be amended. While the original text requires any amendments to be in writing and signed, the added clause allows for amendments based on a 'mutual verbal agreement.' This creates an ambiguity as to whether a verbal agreement can supersede the original written contract, causing potential disputes over enforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties may amend this IP Agreement only by a written agreement signed by the parties and that identifies itself as an amendment to this IP Agreement, unless there is mutual verbal agreement made between both parties.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception that allows for verbal agreements to amend the IP Agreement, directly contradicting the initial requirement that amendments must be in writing and signed. This creates an in-text contradiction because the changed text and the contradicted text are essentially the same, and one states that agreements should be in writing and signed, but then is immediately followed by text that says verbal agreements are sufficient to amend it, which creates an inconsistency regarding the requirements for amending the agreement.

perturbation.original_text: Each party giving any notice required or permitted under this IP Agreement will give the notice in writing, and shall be deemed to have been duly given: (i) when received if delivered personally; (ii) when transmitted if sent by facsimile (with transmission confirmed); (iii) the day after it is sent if sent by commercial overnight courier; (iv) upon receipt if sent by certified or registered mail (return receipt requested); or (v) when transmitted if sent by email (with receipt confirmed by recipient). Notice to a party is effective for purposes of this Agreement only if given as provided in this Section 6.12 at the address of which the sending party has been notified in accordance with this Section 6.12.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party giving any notice required or permitted under this IP Agreement will give the notice in writing, and shall be deemed to have been duly given: (i) when received if delivered personally; (ii) when transmitted if sent by facsimile (with transmission confirmed); (iii) the day after it is sent if sent by commercial overnight courier; (iv) upon receipt if sent by certified or registered mail (return receipt requested); or (v) when transmitted if sent by email (with receipt confirmed by recipient). **However, for routine communications and updates, verbal notifications with email follow-up will be sufficient.** Notice to a party is effective for purposes of this Agreement only if given as provided in this Section 6.12 at the address of which the sending party has been notified in accordance with this Section 6.12.
perturbation.explanation: The modification adds an exception to the 'written notice' requirement by stating that 'verbal notifications with email follow-up will be sufficient' for routine communications. This contradicts the initial requirement that 'each party giving any notice required or permitted under this IP Agreement will give the notice in writing.' It creates ambiguity over what constitutes a 'routine communication' versus a formal notice and whether a verbal notification + email can be considered legally binding.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each party giving any notice required or permitted under this IP Agreement will give the notice in writing, and shall be deemed to have been duly given: (i) when received if delivered personally; (ii) when transmitted if sent by facsimile (with transmission confirmed); (iii) the day after it is sent if sent by commercial overnight courier; (iv) upon receipt if sent by certified or registered mail (return receipt requested); or (v) when transmitted if sent by email (with receipt confirmed by recipient). However, for routine communications and updates, verbal notifications with email follow-up will be sufficient. Notice to a party is effective for purposes of this Agreement only if given as provided in this Section 6.12 at the address of which the sending party has been notified in accordance with this Section 6.12.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception, allowing 'verbal notifications with email follow-up' for routine communications, which directly contradicts the original requirement and the contradicted text that 'each party giving any notice required or permitted under this IP Agreement will give the notice in writing.' This creates a strong in-text contradiction, making it unclear when written notice is required and when verbal communication is sufficient, thus impacting enforceability.
